name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
الٓر ۚ تِلْكَ ءَايَـٰتُ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ ٱلْمُبِينِ (١)
```

```english
Aif, Lam, Ra. These are the signs of the Manifest Book. (1)
```

```arabic
إِنَّآ أَنزَلْنَـٰهُ قُرْءَٰنًا عَرَبِيًّا لَّعَلَّكُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ (٢)
```

```english
Indeed We have sent it down as an Arabic Quran so that you may exercise your reason. (2)
```

```arabic
نَحْنُ نَقُصُّ عَلَيْكَ أَحْسَنَ ٱلْقَصَصِ بِمَآ أَوْحَيْنَآ إِلَيْكَ هَـٰذَا ٱلْقُرْءَانَ وَإِن كُنتَ مِن قَبْلِهِۦ لَمِنَ ٱلْغَـٰفِلِينَ (٣)
```

```english
We will recount to you the best of narratives in what We have revealed to you of this Quran, and indeed prior to it you were among those who are unaware [of it]. (3)
```

## (Commentary)

The purpose of the Surah is to explain the guardianship (الولاية) — *the divine authority and protective care Allah exercises over His servants* — of Allah over His servant who has purified his faith (الإيمان) — *the sincere conviction and submission to truth* — for Him, the Exalted, with absolute sincerity, and whose heart is filled with His love, seeking no substitute for Him and turning toward nothing other than Him. It demonstrates that Allah, the Exalted, takes charge of his affairs, nurturing him with the finest upbringing, leading him to the stations of proximity (القرب), and quenching his thirst from the spring of nearness (الزلفى). Thus, He reserves him for Himself and grants him a divine life, even if outward causes (الأسباب الظاهرة) seem to conspire for his destruction. He elevates him even if events strive for his debasement, and He grants him might (العزة) even if calamities and the misfortunes of time beckon toward his humiliation and the lowering of his status.

The Exalted has clarified this by recounting the story of Yusuf the Truthful (يوسف الصديق) عليه‌السلام. No other story in the chapters of the Noble Quran has been detailed with such comprehensiveness from beginning to end as his story عليه‌السلام, and this Surah has been uniquely dedicated to it without any other story sharing its space.

He عليه‌السلام was a servant sincere in his servitude (العبودية) — *the state of total submission and devotion to Allah* — so Allah reserved him for Himself and empowered him with His might. Although various causes gathered to humiliate and debase him, every time they cast him into a place of peril, Allah, the Exalted, brought him to life through the very same path that was meant to lead him to destruction. His brothers envied him and cast him into the depths of the pit, then sold him for a miserable price—a few numbered dirhams—yet that very path led him to Egypt and entered him into the house of sovereignty and honor. The woman in whose house he resided sought to seduce him and then accused him before the Governor (العزيز), yet it was not long before she confessed his innocence before the women. She then accused him again and had him imprisoned, yet that became the very cause of his proximity to the King. The shirt stained with blood, which they brought to his father Ya’qub on the first day, was the sole cause for the loss of his sight; yet that very shirt, sent by his hand from Egypt to his father on the final day, became the cause for the return of his sight. Such is the pattern throughout.

In short, whenever any opposing cause contended with him or obstructed his path toward perfection (الكمال), Allah, the Exalted, made that very obstacle the cause for the rectitude of his affairs and the success of his quest. The Glorified continued to transform him from one state to another until He granted him judgment and sovereignty, chose him, taught him the interpretation of dreams (تأويل الأحاديث), and completed His favor upon him, just as his father had promised him.

Allah, the Glorified, began his story by mentioning a vision (الرؤيا) he saw at the outset while he was a young boy in his father’s care. A vision is among the glad tidings (المبشرات); He then fulfilled His glad tidings and completed His word regarding him through the divine upbringing He specifically granted him. This is His way, the Exalted, with His friends (الأولياء), as He, the Exalted, says: **أَلا إِنَّ أَوْلِياءَ اللهِ لا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَكانُوا يَتَّقُونَ لَهُمُ الْبُشْرى فِي الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا وَفِي الْآخِرَةِ لا تَبْدِيلَ لِكَلِماتِ اللهِ ذلِكَ هُوَ الْفَوْزُ الْعَظِيمُ** "Look! The friends of Allah will indeed have no fear nor will they grieve—those who have faith and are God-conscious. For them is glad tidings in the life of this world and in the Hereafter. There is no changing the words of Allah. That is the great triumph." (Yunus: 62-64).

In the saying of the Exalted, after mentioning the vision of Yusuf and his father’s interpretation of it: **لَقَدْ كانَ فِي يُوسُفَ وَإِخْوَتِهِ آياتٌ لِلسَّائِلِينَ** "In Yusuf and his brothers there are certainly signs for the seekers" (Yusuf: 7), there is an indication (إشعار) that there were people who questioned the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله regarding this story. This supports the reports stating that a group of Jews sent the polytheists of Makkah to ask the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله about the reason for the migration of the Children of Israel to Egypt, given that Ya’qub عليه‌السلام was residing in the land of Sham; thus, the Surah was revealed.

Accordingly, the purpose is to explain his story عليه‌السلام and the story of the family of Ya’qub. Through His exposition, the Exalted has extracted the supreme purpose therein, which is the manner of Allah’s guardianship (ولاية الله) over His sincere servants, as is evident from the opening and conclusion of the Surah. The Surah is Meccan (مكية), as indicated by the context (سياق) of its verses. The report narrated from Ibn Abbas that four of its verses are Medinan—namely the first three verses and His saying: **لَقَدْ كانَ فِي يُوسُفَ وَإِخْوَتِهِ آياتٌ لِلسَّائِلِينَ**—is refuted by the fact that they are part of a single, unified context.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **الر تِلْكَ آياتُ الْكِتابِ الْمُبِينِ** "Alif, Lam, Ra. These are the signs of the Manifest Book." (Yusuf: 1). The use of the distant demonstrative (تِلْكَ) is for veneration and glorification. It appears that what is meant by the Manifest Book (الكتاب المبين) is this recited Quran, which is manifest and clear in itself, and manifest in clarifying for others the divine gnosis (المعارف الإلهية) and the realities of the Origin and the Return (المبدأ والمعاد) that Allah has placed within it.

The Book is described in this verse as "Manifest" (المبين), unlike His saying at the beginning of Surah Yunus: **تِلْكَ آياتُ الْكِتابِ الْحَكِيمِ** "These are the signs of the Wise Book," because this Surah was revealed concerning the story of the family of Ya’qub and its exposition. It is also possible that what is meant by the Manifest Book is the Preserved Tablet (اللوح المحفوظ).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِنَّا أَنْزَلْناهُ قُرْآناً عَرَبِيًّا لَعَلَّكُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ** "Indeed We have sent it down as an Arabic Quran so that you may exercise your reason." (Yusuf: 2). The pronoun refers to the Book insofar as it contains divine signs and true gnosis. Its being sent down as an "Arabic Quran" means it was clothed, in the stage of descent (الإنزال), in the garment of recitation and the Arabic language, making it a recited word corresponding to the language used by the Arabs, as He, the Exalted, says in another place: **إِنَّا جَعَلْناهُ قُرْآناً عَرَبِيًّا لَعَلَّكُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ ، وَإِنَّهُ فِي أُمِّ الْكِتابِ لَدَيْنا لَعَلِيٌّ حَكِيمٌ** "We have made it an Arabic Quran so that you may exercise your reason. And it is indeed in the Mother of the Book with Us, surely exalted and wise." (Az-Zukhruf: 3-4).

His saying: **لَعَلَّكُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ** "so that you may exercise your reason" is a form of broadening and generalizing the address, for the Surah opens with an address to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله: **تِلْكَ آياتُ الْكِتابِ** "These are the signs of the Book," and it continues in this manner, as in His saying: **نَحْنُ نَقُصُّ عَلَيْكَ أَحْسَنَ الْقَصَصِ بِما أَوْحَيْنا إِلَيْكَ** "We relate to you the best of stories by what We have revealed to you..." (Yusuf: 3).

The meaning of the verse—and Allah knows best—is: We have made this Book, which contains the signs, clothed in the garment of Arabic wording and adorned with its beauty in the stage of descent, so that it may be subject to the exercise of reason (التعقل) by you and your people or your nation. Had it not been cast in its revelation into the mold of recited wording, or had it not been made a manifest Arabic [Book], your people would not have grasped the secrets of the signs within it; rather, its understanding would have been exclusive to you due to your exclusivity in receiving its revelation and teaching.

In this, there is an indication that the words of the Mighty Book—by virtue of their determination through reliance on revelation and their being Arabic—have a role in preserving the secrets of the signs and the realities of gnosis. Had the meaning alone been revealed to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, with the wording expressing it being his own صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله—as in the Divine Narrations (الأحاديث القدسية), for example—or had it been translated into another language, some of the secrets of its clear signs would have remained hidden from human intellects, and the hands of their reason and understanding would not have reached them.

His care, the Exalted, for the wording in what He revealed of His Book is something that no one who contemplates His speech can doubt. How could it be otherwise, when He has divided it into the clear (المحكمات) and the metaphorical (المتشابهات), and made the clear verses the "Mother of the Book" (أم الكتاب) to which the metaphorical ones are referred? He, the Exalted, says: **هُوَ الَّذِي أَنْزَلَ عَلَيْكَ الْكِتابِ مِنْهُ آياتٌ مُحْكَماتٌ هُنَّ أُمُّ الْكِتابِ وَأُخَرُ مُتَشابِهاتٌ** "It is He who has sent down to you the Book. Parts of it are definitive verses, which are the mother of the Book, while others are metaphorical." (Al-Imran: 7). He, the Exalted, also says: **وَلَقَدْ نَعْلَمُ أَنَّهمْ يَقُولُونَ إِنَّما يُعَلِّمُهُ بَشَرٌ لِسانُ الَّذِي يُلْحِدُونَ إِلَيْهِ أَعْجمِيٌّ وَهذا لِسانٌ عَرَبِيٌّ مُبِينٌ** "We certainly know that they say, ‘It is only a human who teaches him.’ The language of him they allude to is foreign, while this is a manifest Arabic language." (An-Nahl: 103).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **نَحْنُ نَقُصُّ عَلَيْكَ أَحْسَنَ الْقَصَصِ بِما أَوْحَيْنا إِلَيْكَ هذَا الْقُرْآنَ وَإِنْ كُنْتَ مِنْ قَبْلِهِ لَمِنَ الْغافِلِينَ** "We relate to you the best of stories by what We have revealed to you of this Quran, though before this you were indeed among the uninformed." (Yusuf: 3). Al-Raghib says in *al-Mufradat*: "The word *al-qass* (القص) means to follow a track; it is said: 'I followed his track' (قصصت أثره). *Al-qasas* (القصص) refers to the track itself; He said: **فارتدا على آثارهما قصصا** 'So they retraced their tracks' (Al-Kahf: 64), and **وقالت لأخته قصيه** 'She said to his sister, "Follow him"' (Al-Qasas: 11)." He continues: "*Al-qasas* also refers to narrated reports that are followed; He, the Exalted, says: **لَهُوَ الْقَصَصُ الْحَقُ** 'This is indeed the true narrative' (Al-Imran: 62), **فِي قَصَصِهِمْ عِبْرَةٌ** 'In their stories there is certainly a lesson' (Yusuf: 111), **وَقَصَّ عَلَيْهِ الْقَصَصَ** 'and he related the story to him' (Al-Qasas: 25), and **نَقُصُّ عَلَيْكَ أَحْسَنَ الْقَصَصِ** 'We relate to you the best of stories' (Yusuf: 3)." [End of quote]. Thus, *al-qasas* is the story (القصة), and "the best of stories" (أحسن القصص) is the finest story and narrative. It is also said that it is a verbal noun (مصدر) meaning the act of narrating (الاقتصاص).

If it is a noun for the story, then the story of Yusuf عليه‌السلام is the best story because it describes the sincerity of monotheism (التوحيد) — *the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts* — in servitude. It exemplifies the guardianship of Allah, the Glorified, over His servant, showing how He nurtures him by leading him along the path of love, raising him from the depths of humiliation to the pinnacle of might, and taking him from the pit of captivity, the tether of slavery, and the prison of punishment and affliction to the throne of honor and the seat of sovereignty.

If it is a verbal noun, then the narration (الاقتصاص) of his story in the manner that the Glorified has narrated it is the best narration, for it is the narration of a story of love and passion in the most chaste and veiled manner possible.

The meaning—and Allah knows best—is: We relate to you the best of stories by means of Our revealing this Quran to you, whereas before Our relating this story to you, you were among those unaware of it.
